claims.description: Product API surfaces expose only their allowed capabilities, resources, router groups, and RPC methods.
claims.title: Product API surfaces are separated

claims.description: Each product UIX has one API base URL and rejects forbidden cross-surface paths.
claims.title: Product UIX clients use isolated API boundaries

claims.description: The Docker public-api dev deployment exposes correct localhost discovery metadata and Swagger request bodies, accepts dev login, registers clients, issues password-grant tokens, redirects authorization-code requests, and exchanges authorization codes with PKCE.
claims.title: Public API dev deployment auth flow proof

claims.description: The Docker resource-validation-api dev deployment exposes correct localhost issuer, JWKS, tenant JWKS, protected-resource metadata, documented introspection form input, and fails closed for public auth and RPC routes.
claims.title: Resource validation API dev deployment metadata proof

claims.description: The Docker platform-admin-api dev deployment exposes the platform control plane with OpenAPI/OpenRPC metadata, admin-only RPC discovery, tenant/user/admin resources, API-key enforcement, and fail-closed exclusion of public auth and developer/client routes.
claims.title: Platform admin API dev deployment control-plane proof

claims.description: The Docker tenant-admin-api dev deployment exposes tenant-scoped control-plane metadata and resources, enforces admin API-key access, permits tenant-scoped RPC methods, and fails closed for platform tenant lifecycle, service-admin, and public auth routes.
claims.title: Tenant admin API dev deployment control-plane proof

claims.description: The Docker developer-api dev deployment exposes dynamic client registration and client-management metadata, enforces admin API-key access for client control-plane routes and RPC, permits developer-scoped RPC methods, and fails closed for platform, tenant, service-admin, authorization, token, login, and introspection routes.
claims.title: Developer API dev deployment client-registration proof

claims.description: Canonical table classes, Base, runtime bindings, and table exports are owned by tigrbl-identity-storage and compatibility imports preserve symbol identity.
claims.title: Identity storage owns canonical table symbols

claims.description: Legacy tigrbl_auth table, ORM, DB, and persistence imports re-export canonical storage symbols without duplicate table definitions.
claims.title: tigrbl_auth compatibility facades preserve storage identity

claims.description: Storage migrations and persistence helpers import canonical storage table and engine symbols directly rather than compatibility facades.
claims.title: Identity storage owns engine, migrations, and persistence helpers

claims.description: Deployable product API frontdoors compose table-backed resources from canonical storage table objects while preserving product-surface fail-closed behavior.
claims.title: Frontdoor APIs compose canonical storage resources

claims.description: Static tests prevent duplicate compatibility table definitions and storage-to-tigrbl_auth table facade imports.
claims.title: Canonical storage import boundary is guarded

claims.description: The My Account consent and authorized-app routes list and revoke only grants owned by the authenticated user.

features.body: Problem`n`nFinal tokens are nondeterministic, and the pre-token decision process is not yet captured as a current governed artifact that explains mutation and verifier-state effects.`n`nExpected behavior`n`nBefore token minting, the system records a normalized authorization-decision artifact with request, policy, derivation, proof-binding, mutation-time provenance, and verifier-time provenance context.`n`nCompletion signals`n`nThe same normalized inputs and policy state reproduce the same decision artifact even when the JWT differs, and the artifact explains outcomes after authority mutation or verifier-state change.
features.description: Record a reproducible authorization decision artifact from normalized inputs, policy state, mutation state, and verifier-state provenance.
features.title: Authorization decision trace artifact

features.body: Problem`n`nAuthorization traces are reproducible before token minting, but the row does not yet state how traces explain outcomes after authority mutation or verifier-state change.`n`nExpected behavior`n`nThe normalized authorization decision trace captures policy, request, proof-binding, mutation-time provenance, and verifier-time provenance so allow and deny outcomes remain explainable when authority changes after issuance.`n`nCompletion signals`n`nThe same normalized inputs and policy state reproduce the same decision artifact, and the artifact remains useful for explaining outcomes after invalidation or verifier-state change.
features.description: Authorization flows preserve deterministic decision traces, including mutation-time and verifier-time provenance that explains allow or deny after authority changes.
features.title: Authorization decision trace reproducibility

features.body:
Problem

There is no stable identifier for a reproducible authorization decision independent of token bytes.

Expected behavior

The decision trace emits a stable provenance hash derived from normalized inputs and policy state.

Completion signals

Repeated evaluation of the same normalized decision produces the same provenance hash.
features.description: Emit a stable authorization-decision hash that can be reproduced independently of final JWT bytes.
features.title: Authorization provenance hash

features.description: Delegation and token exchange preserve durable server-side provenance and lineage beyond request-time claims.
features.title: Delegation graph and token-exchange provenance

features.body:
Problem

Token exchange records immediate actor context but not a durable delegation lineage.

Expected behavior

The system persists governed delegation provenance including parentage, attenuation, proof binding, and issued-token lineage.

Completion signals

Server-side artifacts can reconstruct delegated authority movement without relying only on token payloads.
features.description: Persist durable server-side delegation lineage for token exchange and related delegated authorization flows.
features.title: Delegation graph persistence

features.body:
Problem

Even with durable delegation persistence, downstream audit and verifier tooling need a consumable export shape.

Expected behavior

Governed delegation lineage can be exported for audit, certification, and interoperability review.

Completion signals

A downstream consumer can inspect parent-child delegation history, attenuation, and proof continuity from exported governed artifacts.
features.description: Export governed delegation lineage for verifier, audit, and certification use.
features.title: Delegation lineage export

features.description: Fail closed when preserved authoritative discovery artifacts drift from live runtime-derived metadata.
features.title: Discovery runtime-snapshot drift gate

features.description: Extends discovery snapshot generation to include tenant-scoped examples per profile.

lifecycle.note: Tenant public discovery and JWKS runtime boundary T2 closure.

features.title: Discovery snapshot tenant profile artifacts

features.body:
This feature governs fail-closed evidence hygiene for preserved certification,
release, runtime, and peer-hand-off artifacts.

Scope includes:
- secret scanning before evidence is accepted into proof chains
- redaction-safe projection rules for preserved artifacts
- exclusion of compromised or secret-bearing artifacts from active certification use
- release-gate failure behavior when evidence hygiene checks fail

Preserved evidence must not retain bootstrap credentials, bearer tokens, private
keys, replayable proofs, or similar secret-bearing runtime material. Redacted
markers are allowed only when they clearly indicate that the original sensitive
material is not preserved.

This feature is satisfied only when secret-bearing evidence is blocked from
certification and publication flows unless replaced by clean rebuilt artifacts
from validated execution.
features.description: Fail closed on secret-bearing evidence, require governed redaction markers, and exclude compromised artifacts from active certification proof chains.
features.title: Evidence secret hygiene gates

features.body: Problem`n`nVerifier policy is governed, but freshness, revocation visibility, replay visibility, and stale-state behavior are not stated as first-class verifier obligations.`n`nExpected behavior`n`nEach protected resource verifier contract governs accepted issuers, audiences, token classes, proof requirements, required claims, JWKS freshness, introspection freshness, revocation visibility, replay visibility, and fail-closed behavior when verifier state is stale or ambiguous.`n`nCompletion signals`n`nRuntime verifier behavior and protected-resource metadata can be explained from one governed verifier contract, including how stale or incomplete verifier state is handled.
features.description: Each protected surface defines one governed verifier contract for issuer, audience, token class, claims, sender-constraint policy, freshness inputs, and fail-closed stale-state behavior.
features.title: Protected-resource verifier contract

features.body:
This feature governs the runtime invariant that every issuer-bearing artifact and
every enabled live route derives from the same active resolved deployment object.

Scope includes:
- OAuth and OIDC discovery responses
- protected-resource metadata
- token, ID token, authorization response, token exchange, and introspection outputs
- route enablement and fail-closed route hiding
- TLS posture checks for live request handling
- governed diagnostics and contract-publication surfaces such as `/docs` and `/openapi.json`

Runtime handlers must consume request-scoped or app-scoped resolved deployment
state and must not fall back to global `ISSUER` constants or parallel issuer and
surface decision logic when active deployment state is available.

This feature is satisfied only when runtime artifact posture, route posture,
contract posture, and discovery posture remain deployment-derived and mutually
consistent.
features.description: Derive every issuer-bearing artifact and enabled live route from the same active resolved deployment object, including governed docs and OpenAPI surfaces.
features.title: Request-scoped deployment authority

features.description: Use one effective ResolvedDeployment authority for each live request path.
features.title: Request-scoped resolved deployment authority

features.body: Problem`n`nRFC 9728 metadata can drift from actual verifier behavior if metadata and verifier logic are authored separately, especially around freshness and revocation visibility.`n`nExpected behavior`n`nProtected-resource metadata is derived from the same verifier contract used by runtime verification, including issuer, audience, proof, token-class, freshness, and fail-closed stale-state handling.`n`nCompletion signals`n`nMetadata and runtime verifier behavior remain truthfully aligned for issuer, audience, proof, token-class, and verifier-state expectations.
features.description: Derive RFC 9728 protected-resource metadata from the same verifier contract used by runtime enforcement, including freshness and stale-state semantics.
features.title: Resource verifier contract metadata projection

features.body: Problem`n`nProtected-resource verifier semantics remain distributed across runtime code paths and are not yet modeled as a current governed authority object with freshness semantics.`n`nExpected behavior`n`nEach protected resource has a first-class verifier contract that defines accepted issuers, audiences, token classes, proof requirements, required claims, freshness sources, revocation visibility, replay visibility, and fail-closed stale-state behavior.`n`nCompletion signals`n`nRuntime verifier behavior can be explained and configured from the resource contract rather than endpoint-local logic, including how stale verifier state is handled.
features.description: Define first-class protected-resource verifier contracts as governed runtime authority objects with freshness, revocation-visibility, and stale-state semantics.
features.title: Resource verifier contract model

features.body:
Problem

Public, admin, RPC, and diagnostics planes can still be co-hosted inside one runtime image. Logical route and gate separation is not the same thing as a hard deployment boundary.

Expected behavior

The system supports a hard-isolated plane tier where privileged planes are deployable separately and cannot be mounted accidentally into the public runtime.

Completion signals

Deployment resolution, contracts, and operator-visible posture all distinguish hard-isolated deployments from mixed-mode deployments.
features.description: Govern a hard-isolated deployment class for public, admin, RPC, and diagnostics planes.
features.title: Runtime-plane hard isolation tier

features.body:
Problem

Mixed-mode co-hosting currently looks too similar to a stronger isolation claim.

Expected behavior

When privileged and public planes are co-hosted, the runtime and evidence classify that posture as downgraded rather than fully isolated.

Completion signals

Deployment reports, diagnostics, and release-facing artifacts expose the downgrade explicitly.
features.description: Classify co-hosted multi-plane deployments as downgraded posture with explicit guarantee limits.
features.title: Runtime-plane mixed-mode downgrade classification

features.body:
This feature governs first-class trust-domain authority objects for tenant-aware
issuer, key-selection, issuance, and verification behavior.

A trust-domain authority object must define:
- issuer identity
- JWKS authority
- active and next signing-key scope
- allowed algorithm posture
- verification scope
- cross-tenant rejection behavior

Tenant discovery and tenant JWKS publication are projections of this authority
object and must not drift from issuance or verifier behavior.

This feature is satisfied only when tenant-scoped issuance, signing-key
selection, discovery metadata, and verifier expectations are all derived from
the same governed trust-domain source.
features.description: Resolve issuer identity, signing scope, verification scope, and related trust semantics from a first-class trust-domain authority object.
features.title: Trust-domain authority object

features.body:
Problem

Tenant JWKS publication exists, but key authority still needs explicit trust-domain ownership semantics.

Expected behavior

JWKS publication is driven by trust-domain-owned key scope rather than incidental tenant filtering.

Completion signals

Tenant A JWKS never exposes tenant B material, and rotation behavior stays aligned with trust-domain issuer semantics.
features.description: Use trust-domain authority to drive tenant JWKS publication and token verification scope.
features.title: Trust-domain-scoped JWKS authority

features.body:
Problem

The proposed identity package suite needs a governed package-boundary target for Identity package suite boundary.

Expected behavior

The repository defines the first-wave tigrbl-identity package suite, package ownership, namespace rules, and canonical dependency DAG.

Completion signals

The package boundary is implemented, import-checked, linked to its defining SPECs, and verified by executable boundary tests before promotion beyond planning status.

Planned dependency notes

This feature was proposed with package-DAG dependencies on: feat:identity-import-namespace, feat:identity-package-dag-guards. These remain design constraints through linked SPECs and must become executable import/DAG tests during implementation; they are not formal SSOT requires edges while the package-boundary work is still absent.
features.description: The repository defines the first-wave tigrbl-identity package suite, package ownership, namespace rules, and canonical dependency DAG.
features.title: Identity package suite boundary

The credentials package needs Credential principal reference boundary to be tracked as a targetable first-class feature, not only as a generic credential object.

Expected behavior

Credential lifecycle uses stable principal references and avoids importing concrete principal implementations except for explicit lifecycle validation contracts.

Completion signals

Credential principal reference boundary is implemented in the credentials package, linked to credential-domain contracts, verified independently from OAuth/OIDC flow execution and JOSE token serialization, and produces policy-consumable verification or lifecycle facts where applicable.
features.description: Credential lifecycle uses stable principal references and avoids importing concrete principal implementations except for explicit lifecycle validation contracts.
features.title: Credential principal reference boundary

features.body: Problem`n`nCredential lifecycle rows cover rotation and revocation, but they do not explicitly govern what happens to issued authority after compromise, replay, rotation, or revocation events.`n`nExpected behavior`n`nCredential lifecycle behavior includes revoked credentials, rotated credentials, replay-triggered invalidation, downstream revocation visibility, and fail-closed handling when invalidation state is ambiguous.`n`nCompletion signals`n`nCredential compromise and lifecycle mutations have governed invalidation consequences that can be tested and audited across runtime verification surfaces.

issues.title: Admin gate must defer runtime contract payloads upstream

issues.body:
Problem

The runtime can mint correct tokens while still lacking a durable, reproducible record of the authorization decision that produced them. Token bytes are intentionally nondeterministic, but the pre-token decision process remains under-modeled and difficult to verify independently.

Impact

Without a deterministic authorization-decision artifact, later review cannot reliably answer which inputs, policy version, derived scopes, authorization details, and proof bindings led to a token being issued. This limits auditability, replayable reasoning, and certification of complex authorization flows.

Expected outcome

Create a durable pre-token authorization-decision artifact that records normalized request inputs, policy identity or hash, derivation steps, and a stable decision hash. Tokens can then reference that artifact without becoming deterministic themselves. Completion requires proving the same normalized inputs and policy state reproduce the same decision artifact even though the final JWT still varies by time, jti, and signature.
issues.description: JWT bytes should remain nondeterministic, but pre-token authorization decisions need a durable deterministic artifact with normalized request hashing, policy hashing, derivation steps, and decision linkage that can be reproduced independently of token timestamps and signatures.
issues.title: Authorization decisions need deterministic provenance artifacts before token minting

issues.body:
Problem

Bootstrap admin credentials are being emitted in plaintext during runtime bootstrap behavior and then retained in generated evidence or copied compliance artifacts. This means the same materials intended to prove runtime correctness can also preserve live administrative secrets.

Impact

This is a fail-open control-plane exposure. Any evidence bundle, copied report, retained fixture, or debug transcript containing the bootstrap credential is compromised for certification use and unsafe for broader sharing. The defect also undermines confidence in evidence hygiene because the artifact set can no longer be treated as clean-room proof.

Expected outcome

Remove plaintext bootstrap-key logging, rotate any exposed generated keys, identify and quarantine previously leaked evidence artifacts, and add a fail-closed secret scan gate over preserved evidence outputs. The issue is complete only when generated runtime and evidence flows prove that bootstrap credentials, bearer tokens, private keys, and similar secret-bearing values are never preserved in authoritative artifacts.
issues.description: Resolved by removing plaintext bootstrap-key logging, scrubbing preserved evidence artifacts, and adding a fail-closed secret scan regression over preserved reports.
issues.title: Bootstrap admin key material leaks into logs and preserved evidence

issues.body:
Problem

Current repository posture explicitly says final certification closure is not yet achieved. The remaining blockers include runtime-matrix truth, migration portability confidence, and independently qualifying verifier evidence.

Impact

Without one explicit closure issue, the repo can drift between strong design language and weaker release-state truth.

Expected outcome

Track certification closure as a governed blocker, link it to the missing runtime-truth and independent-evidence surfaces, and keep stronger certification wording fail-closed until the linked blockers are resolved.
issues.description: The repo still states that certifiability is not closed because validated runtime-matrix evidence, migration portability truth, and independent Tier 4 proof are incomplete. That status should be tracked as an explicit SSOT blocker rather than only as narrative in reports.
issues.title: Certification closure remains blocked by runtime matrix truth and independent evidence

issues.description: The CLI metadata declares --config as a runtime/configuration file path, but profile YAMLs are the runtime configuration model. ADR-1065 and SPEC-1087 through SPEC-1089 make --profile the single runtime configuration selector, accepting either packaged profile IDs or external YAML profile paths. Close this issue by removing --config from the generated CLI contract or explicitly deprecating it as a compatibility alias that delegates to --profile without separate semantics, then add validation and negative coverage for duplicate selector usage.
issues.title: --config duplicates --profile and is declared without valid semantics

issues.description: The published admin JSON-RPC table surface method Client.clear returns JSON-RPC error -32603 Internal error even after retrying with schema-shaped params. This is a runtime defect in the generated clear path rather than an expected invalid-params rejection.
issues.title: Client JSON-RPC clear returns internal error

issues.body:
Problem

Static discovery profile artifacts can diverge from live deployment-derived discovery responses. Those snapshots remain useful as fixtures or projections, but they are unsafe to treat as authoritative unless they are generated from the same runtime resolution path.

Impact

Discovery drift causes clients, resource servers, and evidence artifacts to reason about different endpoint URLs, issuer values, or capability flags. That weakens the repository's claim that discovery and runtime behavior share a single governed source of truth.

Expected outcome

Discovery artifacts that are presented as authoritative must be derived from the same runtime deployment resolver used by the app. If a static artifact is only a fixture, it must be clearly labeled and excluded from authoritative parity claims. Completion requires parity tests between live discovery responses and any preserved authoritative discovery artifact set.
issues.description: Static discovery profile snapshots can drift from live deployment-derived metadata. Published discovery artifacts must either be generated from the same runtime deployment resolver used by the app or be clearly marked as non-authoritative fixtures.
issues.title: Published discovery artifacts must be runtime-derived or explicitly fixture-only

issues.body:
Problem

The runtime has a downgrade path where a DPoP-bound access token can pass protected-resource verification without unconditional sender-constraint enforcement on every bearer-token path. The implementation has strong DPoP helpers, but resource enforcement is not uniformly fail-closed.

Impact

This weakens the security value of sender-constrained tokens. A token that should require proof-of-possession can effectively degrade toward bearer semantics if the protected-resource verifier only checks DPoP conditionally. The result is a correctness gap on token replay, proof omission, and proof-binding validation.

Expected outcome

All protected-resource entry points that accept access tokens must enforce sender constraints unconditionally once a bound token is detected. Missing proof, mismatched ath, mismatched confirmation material, wrong presentation type, or replay must all fail closed. Completion requires coverage proving rejection for proofless DPoP-bound tokens, wrong ath, bearer-style presentation of a bound token, and replay across requests.
issues.description: Resolved by making sender-constraint verification unconditional on protected bearer-token paths and proving fail-closed replay and wrong-key behavior in focused security and negative tests.
issues.title: DPoP-bound resource access must fail closed without valid proof

issues.body:
Problem

The repository already references adjacent trust-domain ecosystems such as OpenID Federation and SPIFFE/SPIRE, but it does not yet implement enough runtime and proof semantics to claim those as supported conformance surfaces.

Impact

Without explicit tracking semantics, downstream readers can confuse design intent or research direction with active support.

Expected outcome

Represent these targets as tracked future surfaces, keep them visible in SSOT planning, and prevent certification or README language from implying support before runtime, verifier, and evidence requirements exist.
issues.description: OpenID Federation and SPIFFE/SPIRE are adjacent trust-domain targets, but they are not current claimable conformance surfaces in this repository. The SSOT needs explicit tracked-target rows and wording controls so those standards are not implied prematurely.
issues.title: External trust-domain targets must be tracked explicitly and not claimed early

issues.body:
Problem

The token introspection surface currently allows callers to obtain token state without first proving an authorized caller identity. That permits anonymous or otherwise unauthorized parties to use the endpoint as a token-scanning oracle.

Impact

This is a standards and security failure on a high-value endpoint. Introspection responses reveal token liveness and related state that should only be available to authorized clients or protected resources. Leaving the endpoint open materially increases abuse potential and breaks fail-closed expectations for OAuth resource coordination.

Expected outcome

Require authenticated caller authorization before any introspection result is returned. Supported caller modes can include client authentication or explicitly authorized protected-resource identities, but anonymous access must fail with a contract-valid 401 or 403 response. Completion requires negative tests for anonymous and unauthorized callers plus positive coverage for an authorized caller receiving only the intended token-state fields.
issues.description: Resolved by requiring authenticated introspection callers on the canonical and compatibility RFC 7662 routes, plus unit and integration coverage for anonymous rejection and authorized caller success.
issues.title: Introspection must require authenticated caller authorization

issues.description: The published admin REST table endpoint PUT /logoutstate/{item_id} returns HTTP 500 Internal Server Error for a synthetic UUID probe where the rest of the table surface fails closed. Replace must return a contract-valid response instead of crashing.
issues.title: LogoutState REST replace returns 500

issues.description: Some consumers warn when OpenAPI jsonSchemaDialect is set to a non-default value. Tigrbl Auth contract outputs must omit jsonSchemaDialect, set it to https://spec.openapis.org/oas/3.1/dialect/base, or preserve an upstream Tigrbl-supported OpenAPI 3.1 compatible JSON Schema dialect. The invariant is explicitly tracked and tested.
issues.title: OpenAPI jsonSchemaDialect must be default or omitted

issues.description: The published admin JSON-RPC table surface method PushedAuthorizationRequest.create returns HTTP 204 with an empty body after retry, which violates the expected JSON-RPC response envelope contract.
issues.title: PushedAuthorizationRequest JSON-RPC create returns empty 204

issues.description: The published admin REST table endpoint PUT /pushedauthorizationrequest/{item_id} returns HTTP 500 Internal Server Error for a synthetic UUID probe. Replace must fail closed with a contract-valid status instead of crashing.
issues.title: PushedAuthorizationRequest REST replace returns 500

issues.body:
Problem

Protected-resource metadata exists, but protected-resource verification rules are still distributed across endpoint-specific runtime code. The repository lacks a single contract abstraction from which both metadata and verifier behavior are derived.

Impact

This makes resource behavior harder to audit and easier to drift. Endpoints can accept different issuer sets, audience expectations, proof requirements, or introspection caller rules than those implied by the corresponding metadata. The result is fragmented semantics rather than one governed protected-resource contract.

Expected outcome

Define resource contracts that compile directly into runtime verifiers and RFC 9728 metadata. The contract should own accepted issuers, accepted audiences, token classes, proof requirements, and any introspection-caller policy. Completion requires proving that generated metadata and live verifier behavior are derived from the same contract source rather than separate handwritten logic.
issues.description: Protected-resource metadata is present, but verifier semantics remain distributed across endpoint code paths. Define resource contracts that compile accepted issuers, audiences, token classes, proof requirements, and introspection caller policy into both runtime verification and RFC 9728 metadata.
issues.title: Protected resource contracts must compile runtime verifiers and metadata

issues.description: The published admin REST table endpoint PUT /revokedtoken/{item_id} returns HTTP 500 Internal Server Error for a synthetic UUID probe. Replace must fail closed with a contract-valid status instead of crashing.
issues.title: RevokedToken REST replace returns 500

issues.body:
Problem

Runtime discovery, authorization, token issuance, and token exchange still mix request-resolved deployment state with default or global issuer configuration. That allows discovery metadata, route behavior, JWKS selection, and minted token claims to diverge within the same running deployment.

Impact

Issuer drift breaks trust boundaries, especially for tenant-scoped or profile-scoped deployments. A client or resource may discover one issuer or JWKS location while receiving tokens minted with another authority path. This undermines runtime parity, cross-plane correctness, and the reliability of contract artifacts derived from deployment resolution.

Expected outcome

Use one active deployment resolution path for all issuer-bound behavior: discovery, route enablement, TLS policy, token issuance, token exchange, and any emitted endpoint URLs. Completion requires parity checks showing that discovery issuer, token iss, JWKS URI, and enabled runtime routes all agree for non-default and tenant-scoped deployments.
issues.description: Resolved by routing authorization, token issuance, token exchange, and TLS enforcement through request-resolved deployment state and adding runtime parity coverage for discovery issuer and minted token iss claims.
issues.title: Runtime issuance and discovery must derive issuer and endpoints from active deployment

issues.body:
Problem

The current runtime composes public, admin, RPC, and diagnostics surfaces into one ASGI application. That provides logical separation, but it does not satisfy a harder deployment boundary where privileged planes are isolated by process or trust domain.

Impact

Certification and threat modeling become ambiguous because a deployment can expose multiple sensitivity classes from one runtime envelope. Mixed-mode hosting also makes it difficult to state what guarantees apply to a given deployment.

Expected outcome

Define a hard runtime-plane isolation tier, classify mixed-mode deployments as downgraded posture, and prove what network exposure, authentication, observability, and cryptographic authority each plane may hold.
issues.description: Public, admin, RPC, and diagnostics surfaces are still composed into one runtime and separated mainly by flags, paths, and authorization gates. The repository needs a governed hard-isolation tier plus explicit downgrade classification for mixed-mode deployments.
issues.title: Runtime planes require a hard isolation tier and mixed-mode downgrade classification

issues.description: The published admin JSON-RPC table surface method Tenant.clear returns JSON-RPC error -32603 Internal error even after retrying with schema-shaped params. This is a runtime defect in the generated clear path rather than an expected invalid-params rejection.
issues.title: Tenant JSON-RPC clear returns internal error

issues.description: The published admin REST table endpoint PUT /tenant/{item_id} returns HTTP 500 Internal Server Error for a synthetic UUID probe. Replace must fail closed with a contract-valid status instead of crashing.
issues.title: Tenant REST replace returns 500

issues.body:
Problem

The repository has retained peer-bundle machinery, but the resource-server side of verifier-grade interoperability is still weak because many preserved bundles are not independently qualifying.

Impact

The project cannot honestly elevate protected-resource interoperability to a strong certification claim while peer evidence remains repo-staged or otherwise non-independent.

Expected outcome

Create an independently qualifying protected-resource peer profile, define acceptance rules for that evidence, and block stronger verifier-grade wording until those requirements pass.
issues.description: Resource-server interoperability remains under-proven because current Tier 4 bundles are not yet qualifying as independent verifier evidence. The repo needs a governed independent peer surface for protected-resource verification and replay/sender-constraint behavior.
issues.title: Tier 4 resource-server interoperability requires independent peer evidence

issues.body:
Problem

Token exchange currently captures immediate actor context, but it does not preserve a durable delegation history that records parentage, attenuation, proof binding, and issued-token lineage across exchanges.

Impact

That leaves an audit gap for delegated or chained authorization. The runtime may emit an act claim or log immediate exchange details, but it cannot yet reconstruct a governed delegation graph showing how authority moved, narrowed, or was rebound over time.

Expected outcome

Persist a durable delegation event or graph for token exchange, including parent-child relationships, original and narrowed subject context, proof-binding identity, and issued-token lineage. Completion requires demonstrating that a downstream verifier or auditor can reconstruct the relevant delegation history from governed server-side artifacts instead of relying only on the final token payload.
issues.description: Token exchange emits immediate actor context, but the system lacks a durable delegation graph or event history capturing parentage, attenuation, proof binding, audience or resource narrowing, and issued-token lineage for later verification and audit.
issues.title: Token exchange delegation provenance must be stored as a durable graph

issues.description: The published admin REST table endpoint PUT /tokenrecord/{item_id} returns HTTP 500 Internal Server Error for a synthetic UUID probe. Replace must fail closed with a contract-valid status instead of crashing.
issues.title: TokenRecord REST replace returns 500

issues.body:
Problem

Tenant discovery and tenant JWKS publication exist, but issuance and verification still rely on incomplete runtime semantics for choosing the correct issuer and signing-key scope. The system needs an explicit trust-domain model rather than implicit tenant handling spread across runtime code paths.

Impact

Without a formal trust-domain abstraction, cross-tenant verification boundaries remain too easy to blur. Key rotation, algorithm policy, issuer identity, and tenant-specific signing authority can drift or become difficult to reason about and certify independently.

Expected outcome

Introduce an explicit trust-domain model that governs issuer identity, active and next keysets, allowed algorithms, and tenant-specific verification scope. Issuance, discovery, and verification should all resolve through that model. Completion requires tests proving tenant A and tenant B remain isolated even when hosted by the same root deployment and proving that key selection and issuer semantics remain aligned through rotation.
issues.description: Tenant discovery and tenant JWKS publication exist, but issuance and verification still need an explicit trust-domain model for issuer identity, keyset scope, allowed algorithms, and cross-tenant verification rejection.
issues.title: Tenant trust domain model required for issuer and signing-key selection

issues.description: The published admin JSON-RPC table surface method User.clear returns JSON-RPC error -32603 Internal error even after retrying with schema-shaped params. This is a runtime defect in the generated clear path rather than an expected invalid-params rejection.
issues.title: User JSON-RPC clear returns internal error

issues.body: The admin UIX Tenant JWKS view depends on /tenants/{slug}/.well-known/openid-configuration and /tenants/{slug}/.well-known/jwks.json. In Docker, the admin nginx template does not proxy /tenants/ routes, so discovery returns index.html. The frontend REST helper then swallows the JSON parse failure as an empty object, leaving issuer and JWKS URI blank and requesting /undefined. The fix must proxy tenant discovery/JWKS through admin UIX and make non-JSON REST responses fail loudly.
issues.description: Admin UIX nginx serves /tenants/... discovery routes from the SPA fallback instead of proxying them to the backend, causing the Tenant JWKS view to render blank issuer/JWKS metadata and an /undefined follow-up request.
issues.title: Admin UIX tenant discovery routes fall through to SPA fallback

issues.body:
Problem

The package x Python-version CI matrix now runs the testkit package as the cross-cutting integration lane. On Python 3.10, 3.11, and 3.12, the testkit lane installs and executes, but the runtime integration suite reports the same broad route and profile failures: flat auth, CRUD, device, system, RPC, and documented-profile paths return 404 or tenant-not-found responses where the tests expect mounted runtime behavior.

Observed evidence

GitHub Actions run 26146165581 failed only in the tigrbl-identity-testkit cross-cutting jobs. The Python 3.10, 3.11, and 3.12 jobs report 39 failed, 96 passed, and 3 errors. Representative failures include /register, /tenants, /users, /clients, /api_keys, /services, /service_keys, /system/methodz, /system/healthz, /rpc, /device_authorization, documented-profile endpoints, disabled token-exchange behavior, and DPoP confirmation propagation.

Impact

The isolated package lanes pass, but the cross-cutting proof package shows that the assembled runtime contract is not yet stable under the new split-package matrix. This blocks release confidence because package-local import health is proven while provider-runtime composition is still failing.

Expected outcome

Make the testkit cross-cutting lane assemble the intended provider runtime profile, seed the required tenant/runtime state, and prove the expected public, admin, RPC, device, DPoP, and profile routes across Python 3.10, 3.11, and 3.12. The issue is resolved only when the failing testkit jobs pass in the package matrix run.
issues.description: Python 3.10-3.12 testkit cross-cutting matrix runs tests/integration and tests/interop, but the assembled runtime returns 404 or tenant-not-found responses for expected auth, CRUD, device, system, RPC, DPoP, token-exchange, and documented-profile behavior.
issues.title: Cross-cutting testkit matrix exposes runtime route and profile mismatches

issues.body:
Problem

The package x Python-version CI matrix includes Python 3.13 and 3.14, but the cross-cutting testkit runtime installation fails before pytest starts. The root runtime extras require swarmauri_base==0.9.2, and that published dependency is constrained to Python >=3.10,<3.13.

Observed evidence

GitHub Actions run 26146165581 failed the tigrbl-identity-testkit Python 3.13 and Python 3.14 jobs during pip install -e ".[test,sqlite,postgres,servers]". pip reports that swarmauri_base==0.9.2 has no matching distribution because available versions are ignored by Requires-Python >=3.10,<3.13.

Impact

The repository currently claims and targets Python 3.10 through 3.14 support, but the full provider-runtime testkit lane cannot even install on Python 3.13 or 3.14. This is a release-blocking compatibility gap until the dependency set supports those interpreters or the support claim is explicitly reduced.

Expected outcome

Upgrade or otherwise replace the dependency path so the testkit runtime lane installs and executes on Python 3.13 and 3.14. Since the target support range is 3.10 through 3.14, resolution must preserve those interpreters in the matrix and produce passing testkit evidence for both versions.
issues.description: Python 3.13 and 3.14 testkit cells cannot install the full runtime extras because swarmauri_base==0.9.2 is constrained to Python >=3.10,<3.13.
issues.title: Cross-cutting testkit runtime install blocked on Python 3.13 and 3.14

issues.body:
Problem

The cross-cutting authorization response integration tests use string client IDs such as client123 while the current storage model expects UUID-shaped client identifiers. SQLAlchemy raises a StatementError because the string value cannot be bound to the UUID-backed column.

Observed evidence

GitHub Actions run 26146165581 shows tests/integration/test_authorization_response_types.py failing with SQLAlchemy StatementError: 'str' object has no attribute 'hex' while inserting into authn.clients. The failing parameters include id='client123' and a UUID tenant value.

Impact

The failure blocks the testkit lane independently of route mounting. It also shows contract drift between test fixtures, public client semantics, and storage identifier types after the package split and storage boundary work.

Expected outcome

Align the authorization-response fixtures and client creation helpers with the supported client identifier contract. If public client IDs are intended to be stable strings, add an explicit storage field or alias model for them. If storage UUIDs are authoritative, update fixtures and lookup paths to use UUID identifiers and keep display/client aliases separate.
issues.description: Authorization response integration tests insert string client IDs into UUID-backed client storage, producing SQLAlchemy StatementError before the flow can be verified.
issues.title: Cross-cutting authorization fixtures drift from UUID client storage

issues.body:
Problem

The cross-cutting service-key integration tests start a runtime server but the server does not become ready. The affected tests fail during setup with RuntimeError: server not ready instead of exercising service-key creation and introspection behavior.

Observed evidence

GitHub Actions run 26146165581 shows setup errors in tests/integration/test_full_workflow.py::test_service_key_introspection_flow, tests/integration/test_service_key_creation.py::test_service_key_workflow, and tests/integration/test_service_key_introspection_flow.py::test_service_key_introspection_flow. The captured startup output includes a SQLAlchemy error link ending in /gkpj.

Impact

Service identity and service-key behavior cannot be proven in the cross-cutting package matrix while the runtime fixture fails to start. This leaves a direct gap in the nonhuman identity proof path.

Expected outcome

Fix the runtime fixture startup path and database bootstrap path used by the service-key tests. Resolution requires the service-key creation and introspection tests to reach the running server and pass in the testkit package matrix lane.
issues.description: Service-key creation and introspection integration tests fail during server startup with RuntimeError: server not ready, leaving service identity behavior unproven in the testkit lane.
issues.title: Cross-cutting service-key tests fail because runtime server does not become ready

issues.body:
Problem

The cross-cutting interop tests expect generated Tier 4 external handoff artifacts, but the package matrix testkit job does not generate or package those artifacts before pytest runs.

Observed evidence

GitHub Actions run 26146165581 shows tests/interop/test_tier4_external_handoff_templates.py failing with FileNotFoundError for dist/tier4-external-handoff/external-root-template.json and for the dist/tier4-external-handoff directory.

Impact

Tier 4 interop coverage is present in the testkit lane, but the CI job does not run the required artifact generation step or provide the generated outputs. The resulting failure is a pipeline/product-boundary defect rather than a protocol assertion failure.

Expected outcome

Define whether Tier 4 handoff artifacts are generated test prerequisites, checked-in release artifacts, or test fixtures. Then update the package matrix/testkit lane so those artifacts exist before the interop tests run, and prove the handoff-template tests pass in CI.
issues.description: Tier 4 interop tests fail because dist/tier4-external-handoff and external-root-template.json are not generated or packaged before the testkit lane runs.
issues.title: Cross-cutting interop tests require generated Tier 4 handoff artifacts
